name: Bruneian Nasi Katok
steps: Fry the chicken.
Cook the rice and serve together with the chili powder.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken with the turmeric and garlic, and fry in the oil over medium heat, 5-6 minutes per side, until deeply golden and cooked through.
Cook the jasmine rice in 0.9 cup water, covered, 15-18 minutes.
Make a simple chili paste by frying the chili powder briefly in a little oil.
Serve the fried chicken over the rice with the chili paste alongside.

Nasi katok, meaning roughly 'knock rice' from the practice of knocking on a seller's door for a wrapped takeaway parcel, is Brunei's most iconic everyday meal - simple fried chicken, plain rice, and chili, sold cheaply everywhere.

name: Bruneian Kaya Bread
steps: Toast the bread.
Spread with the butter and preserves.
Serve with coffee or tea.
steps_detailed: Toast the bread until golden and crisp.
Spread generously with the butter, then the preserves (standing in for kaya, the coconut-egg jam common across this part of Southeast Asia).
A simple, sweet breakfast enjoyed across Brunei, reflecting shared culinary ground with Malaysia and Singapore.

name: Bruneian Fried Rice with Egg
steps: Stir-fry the cold rice with the scrambled egg and soy sauce.
steps_detailed: Scramble the egg and set aside.
Fry the garlic briefly, add the cold, day-old rice, breaking up clumps, and stir-fry over high heat 5-6 minutes.
Fold the egg back through, finish with the soy sauce.

name: Bruneian Ambuyat

ingredients.note: ground into starch, as a sago stand-in

steps: Whisk the tapioca starch with hot water gradually, stirring constantly, until it forms a thick, glue-like, translucent paste.
Serve with a tangy dipping sauce made from the lime juice and fish sauce.
Eat by twirling a small amount around a utensil and dipping.

ingredients_full.note: ground into starch, as a sago stand-in

steps_detailed: Grind the tapioca pearls into a fine starch (or use tapioca starch directly if available).

Gradually whisk in hot water, a little at a time, stirring constantly and vigorously, until the mixture turns thick, sticky, and translucent - this is the closest approximation to real sago starch, the traditional base of this dish.
Whisk the lime juice, fish sauce, jalapeno, and strained tamarind pulp into a tangy, sour dipping sauce called cacah.

Serve the starch warm, twirled around a pair of chopsticks or a fork and dipped in the sauce - ambuyat is Brunei's most distinctive national dish, notable for having almost no flavor of its own, letting the dipping sauce do all the work.

name: Bruneian Soto Chicken Soup
steps: Simmer the chicken in the broth with the turmeric until tender, about 20-25 minutes, then shred it.
Cook the noodles separately and combine with the broth and shredded chicken.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken with the garlic and turmeric in the broth for 22-28 minutes until very tender, then remove, shred, and return it to the pot.
Cook the rice noodles separately according to package directions.
Ladle the turmeric-scented broth and chicken over the noodles, top with bean sprouts and cilantro - soto, found across Brunei, Malaysia, and Indonesia, is a beloved turmeric-spiced chicken soup.

name: Bruneian Beef Rendang

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion and turmeric.
Add the coconut milk and simmer, uncovered, 1.5-2 hours, stirring occasionally, until the sauce reduces and darkens.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion, garlic, ginger, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 10-12 minutes.
Add the coconut milk and simmer uncovered over low heat 1.5-2.5 hours, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has reduced dramatically and turned deep brown.
Serve with rice - rendang, celebrated across Brunei and its neighbors, is traditionally reserved for special occasions and festivals.

name: Bruneian Fish Curry
steps: Simmer the onion and turmeric in the coconut milk for 5 minutes.
Add the fish and simmer 10-12 minutes until cooked through.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the onion, garlic, and turmeric in the coconut milk over medium-low heat for 5-6 minutes to let the flavors develop.
Add the fish and strained tamarind pulp, and simmer gently 10-14 minutes until the fish is cooked through and flaky.
Serve with rice - a comforting, tangy coconut fish curry found in coastal Bruneian households.

name: Bruneian Grilled Fish
steps: Rub the fish with the turmeric and garlic.
Grill over medium-high heat, 4-6 minutes per side, until cooked through.
Finish with the lime juice.
steps_detailed: Combine the turmeric, garlic, jalapeno, and half the oil into a paste, and rub thoroughly over the fish.
Marinate 15-20 minutes, then grill over medium-high heat, brushing with the remaining oil, 5-7 minutes per side, until cooked through.
Finish with a generous squeeze of lime juice - grilled fish, often fresh from the South China Sea, is a staple across Brunei.

name: Bruneian Chicken Curry
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion and turmeric.
Add the coconut milk and simmer 20-25 minutes until the chicken is tender.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, and turmeric over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the coconut milk and diced tomatoes, and simmer 22-28 minutes until the chicken is tender and the sauce has thickened.
Serve with rice - a homestyle coconut chicken curry found throughout Brunei.

name: Bruneian Beef Satay

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Marinate the beef with the coconut milk and turmeric for at least 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill until charred and cooked through, 10-12 minutes.
Blend the crushed peanuts with a little water for a dipping sauce.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Marinate the cubed beef with the coconut milk and turmeric for at least 30 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill over medium-high heat, turning occasionally, 12-15 minutes until well charred and cooked through.
Blend the crushed peanuts with the sugar and a splash of warm water into a thick dipping sauce.
Serve the skewers with the peanut sauce and rice - satay is a shared favorite across Brunei and the wider Malay world.

name: Bruneian Beef Stew (Daging Masak Hitam)

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the soy sauce and broth, and simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until the beef is tender and the sauce is dark and thick.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and garlic over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the soy sauce, sugar, and beef broth.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2.5 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce has reduced to a dark, glossy consistency.
Serve with rice - a rich, dark braised beef dish reflecting Brunei's Chinese and Malay culinary crossover.

name: Bruneian Kuih Cincin
steps: Mix the flour with the brown sugar and enough water to form a soft dough.
Shape into small rings and fry until golden and crisp, about 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, brown sugar, baking powder, and enough water to form a soft, workable dough.
Shape into small rings, resembling little donuts.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden brown and crisp.
A traditional Bruneian sweet snack, named for its ring shape.

name: Bruneian Curry Puffs
steps: Brown the chicken and mix with the diced potato.
Make a simple pastry, roll thin, cut into rounds, fill, and fold into half-moons.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the turmeric, then mix with the diced potato.
Make a simple, firm pastry from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into rounds, fill, and fold into half-moons, crimping the edges well.
Fry until deeply golden and crisp, 4-5 minutes - a popular teatime snack shared across Brunei and Malaysia.

name: Bruneian Fried Banana Fritters
steps: Mix the flour with a little water and the sugar into a smooth batter.
Dip the banana slices in the batter and fry until golden, about 2-3 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, sugar, and enough water to form a smooth, coatable batter.
Dip the banana slices in the batter and fry in the oil over medium heat, 2-4 minutes per side, until golden and crisp.
A common, well-loved snack throughout Brunei.

name: Bruneian Fried Tofu with Sambal
steps: Fry the tofu in the oil until golden on all sides, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Toss with the minced jalapeno and serve warm.
steps_detailed: Press the tofu briefly to remove excess moisture, then cube it.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden and crisp on all sides.
Toss with the jalapeno, fish sauce, and lime juice, and serve warm - a simple, savory snack.

name: Bruneian Coconut Sticky Rice
steps: Cook the rice, then stir through the sweetened coconut milk.
steps_detailed: Drain the soaked rice and steam in a cloth-lined steamer basket, 20-25 minutes, until translucent and tender.
Warm the coconut milk with the sugar until dissolved, pour over the warm rice, and let absorb 10 minutes.

name: Bruneian Kuih Bingka

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps: Mix the cassava flour, coconut milk, sugar, and egg into a smooth batter.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until golden.
Cool before slicing.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour

steps_detailed: Mix the cassava flour, coconut milk, sugar, egg, and melted butter into a smooth batter.
Pour into a small buttered dish and bake at 350F (175C) for 32-40 minutes until golden and set.
Cool before slicing into squares - a dense, chewy cassava cake shared across Brunei and the wider region.

name: Bruneian Pengat (Warm Banana Coconut Dessert)
steps: Warm the coconut milk with the brown sugar until dissolved.
Add the banana slices and simmer 6-8 minutes until soft and infused with the sauce.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Warm the coconut milk with the brown sugar over low heat until dissolved, 2-3 minutes.
Add the banana slices and cubed sweet potato (a common addition alongside the traditional taro), and simmer 12-15 minutes until everything is tender and infused with the sweet coconut sauce.
Serve warm - a comforting, simple Bruneian dessert.

name: Bruneian Cacah Dipping Sauce
steps: Whisk the lime juice, jalapeno, and fish sauce together.
Serve alongside ambuyat or grilled fish.
steps_detailed: Whisk the lime juice, jalapeno, fish sauce, and strained tamarind pulp together until combined.
Cacah is the essential tangy, spicy dip served with ambuyat - without it, the starch itself has almost no flavor.

name: Bruneian Sambal Belacan
steps: Blend the jalapeno with the fish sauce and lime juice into a rough paste.
Serve as a fiery condiment alongside rice and grilled dishes.
steps_detailed: Blend the jalapeno, garlic, fish sauce, and lime juice into a rough, fiery paste.
This nods to sambal belacan, which traditionally includes fermented shrimp paste - serve as a bold condiment alongside rice and grilled dishes.
